data.title: Cyclical Value Is, By Far, My Favorite Investment Thesis Going Forward
data.description:
Introduction

I have to admit that I have used some pretty big words in the titles of my articles this year. This includes coverage of paradigm shifts, opportunities of the decade, the Fed’s most dangerous gamble, dividend gems one cannot ignore, and many more.

However, I always stick to my rules, one of which is that I will never clickbait you.

See, as much as I like everyone to read my articles, to me, it’s all about the long game. This includes coverage of big developments and ideas that are truly worth our time and energy, not just articles to get your attention for a few minutes

The reason we have discussed so many big trends recently is that we are truly in uncharted territory. Right now, so many developments in macroeconomics and geopolitics are coming together, creating an environment of both huge risks and tremendous opportunities.

Essentially, all parts of my Big Picture research frame are intertwined.

This includes unprecedented AI disruption, sticky inflation, political uncertainties, elevated deficits, high government debt ratios, a Fed that’s cutting rates with a market at all-time highs, solid GDP growth, and rising inflation, as well as very weak cyclical growth.

  • We cannot view macroeconomic developments without incorporating AI, as its disruptive power is massive. This creates both tailwinds, like higher margins that can justify much higher market valuations, and headwinds, like labor market weakness.
  • Both the Fed and the Trump Administration seem dedicated to broadening economic growth, potentially sacrificing their fight against inflation to kickstart nominal growth.
  • The deficit and elevated debt levels are inflationary. However, they also stimulate economic growth. Lowering deficits is a very tough deal right now.
  • The S&P 500 has become extremely top-heavy, as ten Big Tech stocks dominate the market. While some of these will emerge as AI winners, they are increasingly competing with each other.

These are just four examples that show the issues we discuss almost daily.

In this article, I will cover something that is truly close to my heart, which is a thesis that the market is still completely ignoring. That thesis is the recovery of cyclical value stocks, a segment that has been left in the dust since the end of 2021. Next to AI and energy, this is one of my three biggest ideas for the next few years. It’s also fully tied to the AI buildout.

I am bringing it up for two reasons:

  • New tailwinds are emerging, supported by leading indicators that could finally signal the return of cyclical growth.
  • Many readers have been asking me about this thesis, as confusing signals (which is what I just covered in this intro) make it very hard to see the woods for the trees.

This comment, especially, was interesting to me, as it hit the nail on the head:

From reading this and past articles, the same question keeps popping in my head. It is this. Traditionally, when the consumer stops purchasing "stuff" and the economy moves towards a recession, the rail companies tend to dip as there is not as much "stuff" to transport.

If we move toward this shift in the economy, I would suspect we will experience at least a mild recession before the growth drivers of lower interest rates kick in (by the way, I agree that this will likely fuel an increase in inflation).

So, wouldn't it be wise to wait for this dip in the economy to purchase transportation companies? - SA Comment

It’s so great because we’re indeed in this super weird (for lack of a better description) situation of weakening labor demand, yet solid GDP growth, and a potential recovery in cyclical stocks. It’s a total cluster of headwinds and tailwinds that can lead to big mistakes.

However, I see more tailwinds than headwinds, which is why I am now writing this article.

Deep under the surface could be the start of a trend I expect to supercharge my portfolio, as cheesy as that may sound.

So, let me explain why that is.

A Huge Mess That Needs to Be Fixed

This is one of the most unusual economic environments I have ever encountered. While that statement doesn’t carry a lot of water, given that I started investing in 2010, I think many may agree with me here.

For starters, GDP growth is just fine, as the Atlanta Fed, which uses a wide range of real-time indicators, sees more than 3.0% real GDP growth this year. Without looking at other numbers, this hardly indicates that there’s a need for substantial improvements.

However, as I have written in many articles in the past, the economy has become somewhat divided between AI-related growth and weakness in "traditional" sectors like manufacturing.

For example, the Chicago Business Barometer, which is one of the best leading indicators for manufacturing and industrial output growth, has been below 50 (that’s the neutral line between growth and contraction) for more than 20 consecutive months. This makes it the longest streak without growth since the start of data collection, roughly two decades ago.

Initially, growth exploded after the pandemic, when pent-up demand created massive demand for more goods (and services). However, once initial demand was satisfied and inflation and higher rates started to hurt the economy, these tailwinds were gone.

Since then, growth expectations have been sluggish, which has kept asset managers out of most cyclical industries, with the exception of companies with substantial AI exposure. This includes giants like Caterpillar (CAT) that benefit from secular growth in data center power demand and mining equipment, thanks to high copper and gold/silver prices (see below).

While this is great for Caterpillar, its peers, and my bullish calls on the company, the real issue remains that a big part of the economy isn’t going anywhere. As we can see below, manufacturing production in the United States has gone nowhere for a quarter century.

Needless to say, the U.S. economy has still done well during this period. Services have been booming, Big Tech has taken over the world (more or less), and the S&P 500 has beaten most developed markets.

Meanwhile, under the hood, we see that growth isn’t evenly distributed.

To give you another example, the chart below shows the total return stock price of Union Pacific (UNP), a company I own. The company is such a great indicator because it’s America’s largest public Class I railroad, serving almost every supply chain you can think of. It also has a duopoly in its operating area with Buffett-owned BNSF. And, even more important, it has no internal issues that could pressure its stock price.

Since 2022, investors have made 0% returns. This includes dividends, as it’s a total return chart. Since then, the S&P 500 has returned more than 50%, fueled by Big Tech.

Furthermore, the next chart shows the ratio between the equal-weight S&P 500 and the market-weight S&P 500. The market-weighted S&P 500 is the "normal" S&P 500. Since 2015, large companies have been the main drivers of gains in the stock market. This accelerated after 2022, when Big Tech became the only game in town. I am painting with a super broad brush here, but my point is that with cyclical growth in the tank, it made sense for capital to rush into secular growth stories like AI disruption and related supply chains.

Now, I expect this to change, creating huge opportunities for value stocks.

It’s Time to Bet on a Bottom

If you have been with me for more than a few weeks, you know that this thesis isn’t new, as I wrote articles like "Buried Under Fear: One Of The Best Opportunities I’ve Seen In Years."

The article, which was written in May, was about these developments as well.

Since then, the thesis has been volatile but quite successful, as new developments support my case. This includes a much bigger push of the Trump administration to kickstart cyclical growth, something he has been trying to do by implementing tariffs as well.

Last week, The Wall Street Journal published a piece titled "Trump’s Team Explores Government-Backed Manufacturing Boost," a title I love because it doesn’t waste any time.

According to the article, the administration is looking to use money from a $550 billion investment fund that was established as part of trade negotiations with Japan to invest in the development of semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, critical minerals, energy, ships, and quantum computing.

Because these projects are so critical to the U.S., some would also enjoy preferential treatment through expedited regulatory reviews. We have seen the same in AI.

Below is a paragraph from the White House’s AI Action Plan:

America’s AI Action Plan has three pillars: innovation, infrastructure, and international diplomacy and security. The United States needs to innovate faster and more comprehensively than our competitors in the development and distribution of new AI technology across every field, and dismantle unnecessary regulatory barriers that hinder the private sector in doing so. As Vice President Vance remarked at the Paris AI Action Summit in February, restricting AI development with onerous regulation “would not only unfairly benefit incumbents… it would mean paralyzing one of the most promising technologies we have seen in generations.”2 That is why President Trump rescinded the Biden Administration’s dangerous actions on day one. - White House AI Action Plan

Please note that one of the three key pillars is infrastructure, an area I discuss quite frequently in my articles here on Seeking Alpha. Infrastructure is critical, as it’s the foundation of all key supply chains and the bedrock of innovation. That’s one of the reasons why the Trump administration is now increasingly focused on manufacturing:

If successful, the proposed plan could help the president deliver on his campaign promises. It would also give Trump control over which industries get the benefit of government assistance. Any effort on such a large scale would likely take years - or even decades -to complete. Trump’s successor in the White House could abandon the project, raising questions about whether companies would have enough long-term certainty to sign on. - WSJ

Although it’s fair to make the case that these developments take years to unfold, the tailwinds are strong, as long-term tariffs support economic reshoring. This is fueled by direct investments in plants, infrastructure, and deregulation. Also, this is a bipartisan thing, as the Biden administration supported reshoring as well through, i.e., the Inflation Reduction Act.

Both parties know manufacturing needs to return. They just disagree on how to do it.

That’s why I am quite happy that the WSJ article also noted that American CEOs don’t want more capital. They want regulatory support to turn their plans into reality. I agree with that, as capital doesn’t seem to be an issue. After all, the AI data center buildout alone could provide $8 trillion in CapEx by 2030, a number that will massively support infrastructure (see below).

This brings me to Jordi Visser, a name I have brought up a few times this month, as I share his view on manufacturing PMIs (i.e., the Chicago Business Barometer). In a recent update, he noted that data center demand and massive shortages in bottlenecks like power generation will create a substantial upswing. In his tweet, he wrote that "[...] the bigger story is the AI disruptive boom driving growth; focus on the first, not the trees."

It looks like this thesis is spot on.

For example, the Dodge Momentum Index, which is issued by Dodge Construction Network, saw a 7.5% boost in August to 301.4. That’s up from 100 in 2000 and supported by a surge of 8.9% in commercial planning.

On the commercial side, all sectors sustained momentum over the month – notably led by strength in data centers, warehouses and hotels. Parking garages & service stations also experienced steady gains. On the institutional side, education and healthcare planning decelerated from last month’s growth but remained positive. A slew of detention facility and court building projects also drove lofty gains in the public building sector throughout August. - Dodge Construction Network

This is what the chart looks like:

And the good news continues.

On top of the vertical takeoff in the DMI above, other soft/leading indicators like the Philly Fed Manufacturing Index are showing promising signs.

According to the report, both the general outlook and new orders are now in positive territory, as slightly more than 40% of surveyed companies see higher orders.

As a result, I think the stars are aligning here, creating very fertile ground for a widespread recovery in key indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index, which I am comparing to the Philly Fed index in the chart below. While the Philly Fed index is much more volatile, to me, the trend is clear, which is upside momentum after 20-ish months of contraction:

Moreover, because "everyone" seems to be focused on AI and other high-growth areas, I truly believe this is one of the most overlooked opportunities on Wall Street over the next few years. After all, upswings take at least 1-3 years and tend to come with elevated gains.

More Food for Thought

I don’t buy small cyclical stocks with high volatility. While that is certainly one way to go, I prefer to stick to high-quality dividend stocks. That’s why this part of the article may not come as a surprise to most of my regular readers.

However, as always, I want to give you some food for thought.

Personally, I have spent the past few years boosting my cyclical holdings. Currently, they account for roughly 50% of my portfolio. That also explains why I expect to see substantial alpha in my portfolio if my thesis pans out as expected.

I love high-quality cyclical companies with wide moats. This includes all listed Class I railroads like Union Pacific, CSX Corp. (CSX), Norfolk Southern (NSC), Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), and Canadian National (CNI). These have always been my prime targets to buy before a potential cyclical rebound.

The chart below compares Union Pacific’s year-over-year stock price performance to the ISM Index. During upswings, its shares often rally 40% within 12 months. It has happened at least three times over the past ten years.

Then there are others, like energy companies that produce oil and gas, which are two commodities that tend to see much higher demand during a cyclical rebound. My most recent article on energy was written on September 20, which included three actionable ideas that I am super bullish on.

In general, I think it is hard to go wrong with upstream producers and royalty/land owners like Diamondback Energy (FANG), Devon Energy (DVN), Exxon Mobil (XOM), Texas Pacific Land (TPL), Viper Energy (VNOM), Canadian Natural (CNQ), Cenovus (CVE), and others.

This chart confirms what I just claimed, as it compares energy (XLE) to the ISM Index:

Small-cap stocks are also highly interesting, as they tend to be more cyclical and dependent on low interest rates than big-cap stocks. As we can see below, this applies to energy, communication, health care, and financials, which all have above-average yield spreads.

While small-cap stocks, in general, are not super cheap (see below), their relative valuations haven’t been this low since the early 2000s. This provides a relative advantage in a rotation.

That said, don’t buy companies just because they are small. Keep focusing on healthy balance sheets and competitive moats. The most important thing you can do is to find companies tied to the AI data center buildout, economic reshoring, and similar.

That’s why I love construction, transportation, basic materials, and even financials like banks, money managers, and BDCs.

That’s also why I will provide many more actionable ideas going forward.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

We are truly dealing with one of the most challenging economic environments, as it’s hard to deal with many interconnected variables that all seem to tell a different story.

However, I see one of my biggest theses developing the way I expected, which is the recovery of cyclical value, a sector that has been left behind since 2021/2022.

Soft indicators are slowly pointing at a substantial recovery, supported by secular growth from AI data centers, favorable regulations, tariffs, and lower short-term rates.

Although the ride is bumpy, I expect many cyclical stocks to do extremely well in the years ahead, providing investors with a new opportunity to generate what I expect to become a lot of alpha.

Risks to My Thesis

The biggest risk is a recession or anything that could put the brakes on this thesis, including but not limited to rapid (AI-related) job losses, the inability to provide favorable regulations, runaway inflation forcing the Fed to hike rates again, and factors outside of the United States, including pandemics and the like.

This is one of the reasons why I stick to high-quality cyclical stocks, as they tend to hold up quite well while I'm waiting for the thesis to unfold.

data.title: The New Era Of Strategic Investing: Semiconductors, Rare Earths, And Uranium
data.description:
By Ryan J. Puplava, CMT, CTS, CES

The United States has entered a new phase of industrial policy unlike anything seen in decades. Washington is channeling billions of dollars—through legislation, executive authority, and direct procurement—into domestic supply chains deemed critical to national security. Rare earths, nuclear fuel (announced last week), lithium, drones, specialty defense materials, semiconductors, and dual-use emerging technologies are at the center of this effort.

For investors, this may be more than a passing policy experiment. It could represent what many optimists believe will be a lasting structural reallocation of capital—one that may create winners in key industries. Below is a breakdown of the primary programs, their financial commitments, and the investment implications.

How Washington Directs Capital

The backbone of America’s new industrial strategy rests on five powerful policy and funding vehicles. The CHIPS and Science Act of 2022 allocates over $52 billion to reshore semiconductor manufacturing, research, and workforce development, ensuring the U.S. can meet national security and economic needs without relying on foreign supply chains (think Taiwan). The Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) complements this effort with tax credits and subsidies designed to strengthen clean energy, critical minerals, and advanced manufacturing. The Defense Production Act (DPA), Title III, gives the Department of Defense authority to provide grants, loans, and long-term purchase agreements to shore up domestic production of materials deemed vital to defense readiness. Meanwhile, the Department of Energy’s Loan Programs Office leverages federal credit to finance large-scale energy, nuclear, and critical mineral projects that private lenders might consider too risky.

Finally, the Defense Logistics Agency’s National Defense Stockpile is being rebuilt after decades of neglect, with renewed purchases of rare earths, specialty metals, and other strategic materials to secure supply for future military and industrial needs. CHIPS and Science Act (2022): Over $52 billion allocated for domestic semiconductor manufacturing, R&D, and workforce training. The recent One Big Beautiful Bill Act also is helping to allocate $2 billion to the Department of Defense for enhancing the National Defense Stockpile.

Taken together, these tools allow Washington to shape entire supply chains—from mining through advanced manufacturing—while providing anchor demand for private industry.

Semiconductors: The Foundation of Modern Security

Semiconductors are the flagship of America’s new industrial push. The Commerce Department and NIST are already deploying nearly $39 billion in direct awards under the CHIPS for America program to fund new fabs, research centers, and equipment plants.

While chip foundries capture headlines, the ripple effects extend downstream. Makers of lithography equipment, deposition tools, wafer materials, and specialty chemicals could stand to benefit from sustained capital expenditures.

Importantly, CHIPS subsidies come with “guardrails”: firms that accept U.S. subsidies are restricted from expanding advanced capacity in certain foreign jurisdictions. For investors, this reinforces the need to focus on domestic suppliers and toolmakers positioned to capture a larger share of this reshaped global supply chain.

Rare Earths and Critical Minerals

America’s reliance on overseas sources of rare earths, magnets, and strategic minerals has long been recognized as a vulnerability. Washington is now moving decisively.

  • Pentagon grants and loans—hundreds of millions of dollars via DPA Title III—have gone to companies like MP Materials (MP) to build domestic separation and magnet production capacity.
  • The Defense Logistics Agency has resumed purchases for the National Defense Stockpile, including bismuth and other specialty metals vital for defense.
  • The Department of Energy has launched multibillion-dollar, multi-year initiatives supporting mineral processing, recycling, and new refining capacity.

For investors, the real opportunity may be in the midstream: processors and magnet makers with high barriers to entry, especially those with government purchase agreements that reduce project risk.

Building a Strategic Uranium Reserve

Nuclear energy is back in focus—especially domestic uranium mining, enrichment, and fuel cycle capacity. The Department of Energy has created a Strategic Uranium Reserve, now actively purchasing uranium from U.S. producers. The first Trump administration tried to buy $150 million worth of uranium in 2020. In May 2024, Biden signed a law to begin moving away from our Russian dependency for uranium. Reuters recently wrote that the U.S. is far behind its peers with only 14 months of uranium inventory, while Europe has two and a half years. China has 12 years worth of inventory of uranium to supply its current needs.

DOE is also supporting advanced reactor designs and enrichment facilities through its Loan Programs Office and Civil Nuclear Credit program.

For investors, the thesis seems clear: uranium miners and fuel fabricators with U.S. operations may have the government as an anchor customer. This could create both a price floor and a financing backstop, improving project viability and long-term returns.

Drones and Autonomy

Unmanned systems have moved from experimental projects to frontline priorities. DARPA and the armed services are investing heavily—often in the hundreds of millions—into drone swarms, autonomy, and counter-drone technologies.

Small drone manufacturers, sensor developers, and autonomy software companies are particularly attractive, as their prototypes can quickly mature into full procurement programs. Unlike many areas of defense, this is a space where small- and mid-cap firms can compete with primes, as technology adoption cascades rapidly.

Investors who monitor DARPA solicitations and Air Force or Army contract announcements may be able to identify promising companies early, before they achieve mainstream recognition.

Specialty Metals: Restocking the National Defense Stockpile

After decades of neglect, the National Defense Stockpile is being rebuilt. The Defense Logistics Agency has begun purchasing metals like titanium, tungsten, and bismuth through multi-year, multi-million-dollar contracts.

This benefits not only mining and refining companies but also the aerospace, electronics, and munitions manufacturers who rely on secure inputs. Because the global supply base for many specialty metals is narrow, even modest DLA contracts can create material demand spikes for niche producers.

Investors should track DLA solicitations closely, as they often highlight under-the-radar companies positioned for sudden growth.

Investment Implications: Following Federal Flows

For investors, Washington’s industrial policy wave may provide signals related to certain strategies:

  1. Track solicitations and funding calls. NOFOs (Notices of Funding Opportunity) and RFPs (Requests for Proposals) often precede major contract awards.
  2. Differentiate up- and downstream. While miners benefit, midstream processors and manufacturers may offer better risk-adjusted returns.
  3. Seek offtake agreements. Government purchase guarantees, like MP Materials’ DoD partnership, may lower project risk and stabilize revenue.
  4. Boosting Uranium Reserves. DOE uranium purchases and enrichment contracts could provide direct, immediate revenue streams to domestic producers.
  5. Drone Innovation. Small defense and drone innovators could represent significant opportunities as contracts expand.
  6. Mind the guardrails. Export controls and domestic sourcing requirements may determine which companies capture subsidies.

Conclusion: Investing in Washington’s Protectionism

The U.S. government is no longer content to rely on global markets for critical inputs. By combining legislation, executive authority, and direct procurement, Washington is rebuilding domestic capacity across semiconductors, rare earths, nuclear, drones, specialty metals, and artificial intelligence.

For investors, the opportunities could lie in identifying the companies aligned with federal commitments. Those that secure grants, contracts, or offtake agreements may stand to benefit from stable, long-term demand. These are themes I am exploring for my growth clients’ allocations, including drone companies when the opportunity presents itself. By positioning ahead of these flows, investors may capture much of the upside of America’s industrial rebuild.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Microvast Doubles Down In Huzhou: Why China Is Now The Core Growth Engine
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my previous coverage of Microvast (NASDAQ:MVST), we were heading into 2Q25 earnings. Now, we're seeing the company go through a bit of change, from a niche commercial EV battery supplier to a very flexible cell and pack supplier for multiple OEM markets in Asia. Since the cancellation of the Clarksville grand, they have turned their efforts towards Huzhou, China, with the expansion of their Phase 3.2 plant. The company is set to dedicate a whopping 2 GWh of new capacity to its HpCO 53.5 Ah NMC cell production line.

It's a very capable and flexible cell line that can cater to OEMs of buses, trucks, and even specialty construction vehicles. In my opinion, this shift to China and a dedicated flagship cell is a wise move by Microvast. I'll explain why later on. The market is also optimistic about this move, which is why the stock has seen considerable upward movement, increasing by about 33% over the last month. The other bit of news investors liked recently is that multiple battery production plants across the US were raided by ICE this month; however, Microvast's plant wasn't one of them. Financially, we are just seeing this shift start to play out, which makes me bullish going forward. Management guided for a midpoint of 21% revenue growth in 2025, and last quarter, we saw them increase their gross margin guidance to 32%. I see that operating leverage from the Huzhou rollout is materializing considerably in the mid-term.

Since my last buy call, the stock is up roughly 8%. My mistake was publishing the call slightly too early, as the stock fell into the mid $2 range, which would have been the best time for investors to buy in. Now, with the stock running up to the $4.50 level, I don't see the need for investors to rush in at current levels, which is why I am downgrading my call from a strong buy to a buy.

2Q25 Results

The recent earnings results were a mixed picture for me. We saw some progress on profitability but also some weakness at the top level. The company posted a non-GAAP EPS of $0.05, beating estimates by about $0.03. Revenue for the quarter hit $91.3 million and was well below expectations, missing out on consensus by about $15 million. On the profitability side, they made good progress, improving gross margins to 34.7%, up from 32.5% a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA swung positive at $25.9 million, up from a loss of $78.4 million this time last year. On a non-GAAP basis, the company turned out an adjusted net profit of roughly $16.3 million. This figure looks good compared to the steep loss last year. However, GAAP net losses widened slightly to $106.1 million. The balance sheet also looked quite strong, with cash and equivalents at $138.8 million, up from both December last year and the year-ago period.

A Chinese Expansion

Microvast is rolling out its phase 3.2 program in Huzhou, China. It's tipped to add about 2 GWh of annual capacity and is targeted specifically for the high-energy NMC 53.5 Ah production runs. For those who don't know, that's their nickel, manganese, cobalt battery family. But what does this rollout mean exactly? Well, we're seeing a shift from Microvast using multiple battery chemistries to eventually just a flagship cell. The cell is, of course, the building block of the battery pack, and the NMC 53.5 Ah is its best contender to take that flagship lead. In terms of capacity, 2 GWh is quite large.

To put that figure in context, it can power about 40,000 electric buses, each with a 50 kWh pack, or 25,000 medium-duty trucks with 80 kWh packs. So just keep in mind that Microvast's Huzhou Phase 3.2 means the factory can now make 2 billion watt-hours worth of cells annually, a massive boost to output. So back to the NMC cell, it's a pouch cell that should be capable enough for buses, trucks, and any other energy storage systems. By dedicating the new production lines to this one cell, they will avoid numerous machinery switches, and the production chemistry will now be uniform. This move is obviously more efficient for the company, and should boost battery yields and reduce that all-important unit cost figure. Microvast has seen strong demand in recent years for NMC cells, which have roughly 235 Wh/kg energy density, so a lot more energy per kg than other cells. The pack also charges quite quickly, which makes it a particular favorite of fleet vehicles that want their equipment out working for as much of the day as they can.

So why switch to a flagship cell? Well, for a long time, Microvast had planned to have a big footprint in the US. The US production effort was supported by a $200 million DOE grant initially awarded under the Inflation Reduction Act battery program. It involved the construction of a battery plant in Clarksville, Tennessee. However, a few years ago, the grant was withdrawn amid pushback from Microvast's ties to China. So the Clarksville protection was put on hold. The proposed plant was tipped to focus on Lithium Iron Phosphate. It's a chemistry much better suited for stationary energy storage systems and North American utility customers. Here in North America, they tend to care a lot more about safety, cycle life, and cost rather than pure energy density, like we see in the Chinese market.

Without proper funding, Microvast turned its attention to Huzhou instead, where construction is set to be completed this year. Back on the 2Q earnings report, management mentioned they expect to finish installation and commissioning of production equipment at the site by the end of 2025, and it seems they were seeing them keep on that promise. In my opinion, this is the better area for management to focus on; they won't have to start from scratch here. In Huzhou, they already have local suppliers for cathodes, anodes, and separators, in addition to existing customers in the bus, truck, and highway vehicle markets.

A Dedicated Production Line

Within the NMC family, Microvast sees the HpCO 53.5Ah NMC pouch cell as the epicenter of Huzhou's incremental capacity. The specs of this cell are exceptionally well-suited for fleet users and energy storage needs. The cell can hit 80% capacity in just under an hour of charge, so it's no surprise we're seeing demand here from those wanting an EV vehicle in heavy use every day. Keep in mind here that the Huzhou plant isn't just for making cells; Microvast runs cells, modules, and trays with thermal plates for duty cycle extremes. So by pairing the 53.5 Ah cell with pack families like the MV-I, MV-B, and the MV-C. With these kinds of packs, they can tailor the voltage and current to OEM envelopes. Whether clients come in seeking batteries for 600V buses or 400V buses, Microvast can serve them. This kind of flexibility is essential if they want to ramp up OEM adoption. It can cut out years from OEMs co-developing a custom pack with the company before a contract deal is made. The packs, despite being adaptable, all meet the required safety needs and also hit the thermal standards, so whether it's a roof-mounted bus pack or an under-floor tray, Microvast can fit them.

China is still the world's EV center, and we often see commercial electrification expand to passenger EVs, where vehicles see price battles. By concentrating their NMC line in Huzhou, Microvast couldn't be closer to the Chinese bus/truck OEMs that are looking for power. A site in Huzhou also keeps lanes open for EMEA integrators. Remember, this segment made up 43% of revenue in 2Q25. The company also raised its FY25 gross margins guidance to 32% last quarter. For me, the main push behind this raise is this single-cell focus we're seeing. Microvast wants much lower conversion costs moving forward, so their new lines are all built for repeatability. We see the same die sets, the same forming processes, and uniform protocols across the site. On top of this, the plant is using Microvast's standard cooling plates or BDU interfaces when laying the pack. And while this may seem trivial, it helps reduce BOM inflation.

Targeting A Triple Revenue Lag

The way I like to view this new expansion is that the Huzhou Phase 3.2 is a 53.5 Ah NMC foundry which feeds three very specific revenue lags for Microvast. Firstly, for commercial EV packs, energy density matters a lot for buses on routes that need longer stints between depot charges. So if we pair the HpCO with the MV-1 pack, we can get up to 70 kWh per unit. On top of that, if the OEM opts for thermal/BMS tuning, they can get a battery with a very impressive life cycle.

The second revenue lag would have to be specialty vehicles. These would be off-highway vehicles, so those used by municipalities or mining companies, for instance. In this revenue sector, where clients need more durable thermal management, a much higher peak current is required for shorter bursts. What I mean by this is that these vehicles aren't cruising at highway speeds like buses; they need to stop, start, and lift or climb harsh terrain. That means the battery needs to handle those high current bursts and frequent cycling. All this creates a lot of heat inside the cell, and OEMs are at a point in their learning now that this can age cells unevenly and lower the battery's lifespan. So, the HpCO is liquid-cooled and has thermal plates to pull that heat out evenly. But the other big advantage here from having the Huzhou site is that cells produced in the same batch have nearly identical electrical characteristics with regard to capacity and resistance.

With OEMs taking cell orders from the same batch, they can use them immediately in module assembly, resulting in a lower impedance mismatch across cells. This ties in with the third revenue lag, offering energy storage system options to clients. Microvast has now been porting the 53.5 Ah cell into ESS blocks. Now, these blocks need high-energy density if they're to be used for MWh-scale projects later down the line. So with the Huzhou plants running large, consistent batches, it simplifies any module binning and pack assembly for the racks.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, Microvast stock still appears slightly expensive relative to the sector. That premium is telling us something about what investors are pricing in. On a forward EV-to-sales basis, the stock is trading at a ratio of 3.18, nearly 47% higher than the sector median of 2.16x. Similarly, its forward price-to-sales multiple of 2.53 sits at a 52% premium to the sector's 1.67. It seems investors are clearly willing to pay a bit more for Microvast's revenue than they are for the average peer. The market has an expectation that Microvast will continue to grow revenue, and after the Huzhou rollout, I don't see them being wrong. Management is guiding for 18% to 25% top-line growth in 2025 and a margin expansion story supported by its Huzhou Phase 3.2 scale-up. Importantly, the premium also prices in Microvast's positioning as a very niche player in the Asia market with proprietary NMC technology. The tech is fast-charging cell chemistries, and the company's geographic advantage is insulated from US tariff exposure.

What's Next?

Now in 2H25, I'm keeping a close eye on the development of the Huzhou site. I'm looking out for any early production metrics next earnings, particularly about yield at the site. Long-term, we should watch to see if the companies' China footprint grows further. I think the Clarksville project isn't coming back, and with tension rising now between the two, Microvast is starting to choose sides.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Intel And Apple: A Deal In The Making?
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Intel (INTC) looks to Apple (AAPL) as Tan seeks to bring shine back to chipmaker: report. (00:24) Former energy secretary Rick Perry's data center REIT launches IPO roadshow. (01:28) Passenger car sales rise in EU; BYD outshines Tesla (TSLA). (02:23)

This is an abridged transcript.

Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) has allegedly approached Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) to consider investing in the company.

According to Bloomberg, Apple and Intel have also discussed fostering a closer relationship.

The report cites people familiar with the conversations. However, the talks are reportedly in a very early stage and may not materialize into action.

Intel jumped 6.4% by the close of market trading on Wednesday. INTC is up 3.4% in premarket action.

Intel’s CEO has helped the company secure a flurry of massive investments recently. This includes a $2B investment by Japan's SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY)(OTCPK:SFTBF), a 10% stake by the U.S. government and, most recently, Nvidia's (NVDA) $5B investment.

Apple, at one time, was a major customer for Intel. It began using Intel microprocessors in its Mac computers back in 2006. However, in 2020, Apple started to transition away from Intel. The transition was complete by 2023.

Data center REIT Fermi (FRMI) announced on Wednesday the launch of a roadshow for its proposed IPO.

The IPO is for 25M shares, expected to be priced between $18 and $22 per share.

Fermi was co-founded by the former U.S. Energy Secretary Rick Perry and the co-founder of Quantum Energy (OTCPK:QREE), Toby Neugebauer.

The company develops electric grids that deliver highly redundant power at gigawatt scale, required to create next-generation artificial intelligence.

A registration statement relating to the securities has been filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, but has not yet become effective.

Also called Fermi America, the company intends to grant the underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 3.75M shares at the IPO price.

The company intends to list its shares on the Nasdaq Global Select Market and the London Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol FRMI.

Passenger car registrations in the European Union climbed by 5.3% Y/Y to 677,786 units in August 2025, following a 7.4% gain in July, the European Automobile Manufacturers' Association (ACEA) said.

This marked the 2nd consecutive month of growth, mainly driven by increases in three of the EU's four largest markets: Spain (17.2%), Germany (5.0%), and France (2.2%). Conversely, registrations in Italy declined by 2.7%

On a monthly basis, August saw Chinese carmaker BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF) outsell Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) in the region for the second consecutive month. BYD achieved a 201.3% Y/Y surge in sales, capturing 1.3% of the EU market, while Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) sales plunged 36.6%, leaving it with just 1.2% market share.

While the company with the highest market share in the area, Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) saw a 6.3% Y/Y rise, with the market share in August recorded at 28.1%.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Qualcomm unveils new Snapdragon chips for mobile, PCs at summit

Lithium Americas surges 95% on report of possible U.S. government stake but 'clock is ticking'

Delta said to replace engine units on A320 fleet to address toxic fume leaks

Catalyst watch:

  • Pure Storage (PSTG) will hold its Pure//Accelerate NYC event. CEO Charlie Giancarlo and founder John Colgrove will both give talks, and there will be a special meeting for financial analysts.

  • Notable investor events include Okta's (OKTA) Investor Meeting, Klaviyo's (KVYO) Investor Day, and Malibu Boats (MBUU) Investor Day.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is down 1% at $64/barrel. Bitcoin is down 1.3% at $111,000. Gold is up 0.5% at $3,754.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.3% and the DAX is down 0.5%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Transocean (NYSE:RIG) -13% - Shares slumped after the offshore driller priced a public offering of 125M shares at $3.05 each, larger than the 100M shares originally planned.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:20 am Chicago Federal Reserve Bank President Austan Goolsbee will participate in a moderated question-and-answer session before Crain's Power Lunch,"The Fed and the Economy: Trends for West Michigan"

  • 8:30 am GDP

  • 9:00 am Kansas City Federal Reserve Bank President Jeffrey Schmid will speak on "The View from Main Street" (monetary policy, economic and banking outlook) before the Mid-Sized Bank Coalition of America.

  • 10:00 am Existing Home Sales

  • 10:00 am Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman will speak on "Supervision and Regulation" before hybrid Financial Markets Quality Conference 2025.

  • 1:00 pm Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr will participate in "Bank Stress Testing" conversation before the Peterson Institute for International Economics.

  • 3:30 pm San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in conversation before the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco 2025 Western Bankers Forum.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PML: Offering Multi-State Muni Exposure At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
Main Thesis & Background

The purpose of this article is to evaluate the PIMCO Municipal Income Fund II (NYSE:PML) as an investment option at its current market price. This fund's objective is to invest "at least 90% of its net assets in municipal bonds whose interest is exempt from regular federal income taxes," and the fund invests at least 80% of its net assets in bonds that at the time of investment are investment-grade quality - bonds that are unrated but of similar quality.

I have covered PML many times over the years, but it has been a while since my most recent review, which was in December 2024. At that time I saw some value in the fund, but—in hindsight—its performance has actually been quite mediocre since publication. While this CEF has seen a sharp uptick in the very short run recently, its trend over most of the year was negative, which has left it with just a slight gain as a result:

Given this weak return in an otherwise strong market, I figured it was time to take another look at PML. While a gain is still a gain, it is important to consider if better value is to be had elsewhere, as would have been the case since last year. However, I still like what I see when I evaluate this fund. The merger with other PIMCO CEFs is now over, the fund still has a discount to NAV, and its tax-exempt yield remains enticing. I will cover these attributes and more in this article to explain why I feel keeping the "buy" rating in place makes sense.

Merger Headwind Is Over

To begin, I will look at a factor that I believe held back PML for the first half of this year. It has to do with the reorganization of a number of PIMCO muni CEFs by the fund manager. There is nothing inherently "wrong" or "bad" with doing this—in fact, it can often be a positive for future returns. But the uncertainty regarding what this means for holders of the fund or funds in question can lead to selling pressure that may not have otherwise occurred.

My point is that when investors—whether current or potential shareholders—are uncertain about what a management decision will bring, that could cause them to sell their shares or stay on the sidelines. In this case, PML was set to absorb both the PIMCO Municipal Income Fund (PMF) and the PIMCO Municipal Income Fund III (PMX), which did occur in August. Both of those funds are now "defunct" and their assets have been rolled into PML, as the following press release indicates:

The good news is that now this headwind is gone because the reorganization has become a reality. PML continues to trade while the others do not - and a similar story can be seen within the New York and California muni funds that PIMCO offers (although they are not the subject of this article).

In my opinion, this helps the outlook going forward because the size of the fund has grown (given that it took ownership of the assets of the other two funds) and that has often resulted in lower spreads and (sometimes) lower fees over time. That is central to why I continue to view PML as a buy here.

But to reiterate, this was a challenge for investors throughout 2025 because they may not have been sure if the proposed merger would either go through or, if it did, would be profitable for them. It also led some investors to try to pick and choose entry points in and out of these funds to try to capture wider discounts, which would ultimately narrow when the merger was completed. This overall backdrop was negative for the fund, as the performance this year shows. But now this environment has changed—for the better, I believe—and we can put this reorganization in the rearview mirror for good.

Discount To NAV Still Present

My followers know I love muni CEFs at a discount. In fact, I rarely buy them at a premium or even at par. Plenty of opportunities to buy munis at a discount through these funds have existed throughout my investment journey, and today is no different. So I see little incentive to deviate from this course.

In the case of PML, this is as true today as it was back in December. While the discount has narrowed a bit, it is roughly at the same spot it was back then, which makes me confident that this is a reasonable spot to pick up the fund:

Of course this doesn't guarantee a strong return (or even a positive one!). PML has pumped out a fairly paltry 1.5% return since last December on the backdrop of a similar valuation, and that isn't too impressive given the risk-free rate is three times that.

But I would venture that today's market has very few offerings in terms of "value" as gold, equities, non-US stocks, and even cryptocurrencies are sitting near all-time highs. With munis only starting to make a comeback recently and PML sitting with a discount to NAV, I think this offers readers a splash of value in a market that otherwise lacks it.

Income Metrics Aren't The Brightest

While I laid out a couple of tailwinds thus far, I do want to point out there are risks to picking up PML here. Chief among them is the sustainability of its income stream. On the surface its current yield of around 6.5% looks like a slam-dunk decision, especially once we consider the increase in the real yield once tax-savings are factored in:

However, in my view, a high yield is only as good as its likelihood of continuing. This is where I differ from some other writers and readers on this site. Just being "high" isn't enough to entice me - it has to be sustainable too.

That is where the problem with PML could potentially lie. Its current income metrics aren't terrible, but they don't give me a lot of confidence either:

With coverage ratios in the 80% range, that signals to me that something will have to change. The fund could see its net investment income rise or it may have to cut what it pays out monthly. If it does, the share price is likely to see some volatility. So those considering a position in this fund need to weigh that risk.

A reason I am still comfortable staying bullish on this fund is because I think income metrics will improve in the months ahead because we are post-merger. The fund is larger and thus has the potential to lower expenses and potentially extract better terms on future securities buys. In this way the merger could help PML boost its coverage ratios and remove this important headwind. Time will tell, and I shall be monitoring these metrics very closely going forward.

I Prefer The Multi-State Approach

My final point on PML is with respect to the fund's holdings. While I often cover single-state funds, I live in a state (North Carolina) that doesn't exactly dominate the muni sector. Finding a CEF that has North Carolina issues is a bit of a challenge, and I have therefore been a believer in either buying individual issues from the state directly, or buying multi-state CEFs to diversify my portfolio. PML can fit the latter objective because it is not too heavy in any one particular state or region of the country:

I think this broad exposure can serve the right investor well. This is especially true for individuals - even if they are in upper tax brackets federally - that don't have significant state taxes. If that is the case, buying munis from just their home state has limited benefits and presents concentration risk. Utilizing a multi-state approach (such as through PML and similar funds) removes that concentration risk.

A key factor in why I am willing to take a multi-state approach is that, in aggregate, state and local governments have a healthy fiscal position. After years of federal stimulus that flowed to states, larger-than-anticipated tax receipts, and the ability to lock in low borrowing costs for the long term, state governments are in a strong position. This is evidenced by the expansion of so-called "rainy day" funds - which are reserves that can be tapped to fill budget gaps as they arise:

The conclusion for me is that muni debt is a smart place to put down cash at the moment. State and local governments continue to see low level of defaults and the high amount of reserves tells me that is going to continue for the time being. Given that my federal tax rate is significantly higher than my state tax rate, I see value in removing concentration risk and shifting some of my cash to a fund that owns a variety of states (as opposed to only my home state). This helps support my interest in PML.

Bottom-Line

Muni bonds are on my radar, and PML is a reasonable option here. The fund's high yield, discount to NAV, and diversified portfolio are all positive attributes. However, readers should consider the mediocre interest coverage and underperformance over the past year before getting too aggressive. Further, interest rate volatility is starting to pick up, which could pressure bonds more broadly:

Of course, as rates decline, that should help boost the prices of the underlying securities. But more volatility is something I would urge my followers to understand and be sure they are able to handle it. If so, then I would suggest giving PML some consideration going forward.

data.title: LDP: CEF Focused On Preferred Securities
data.description:
Overview

Cohen & Steers Limited Duration Preferred and Income Fund (NYSE:LDP) is structured as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns from its portfolio of preferred securities and fixed income assets. Income funds like LDP can be an efficient way to offset the market volatility and uncertainty that traditional equities can expose investors to. LDP has an inception dating back to 2012, which means we have over a decade worth of operating history to reference. LDP's expense ratio is listed as high as 3.75%, but this is deceiving because it includes the fund's operating and interest expenses. The actual management fee sits closer to 0.7% of the fund's assets.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that LDP's share price has increased by about 2.3%. The fund has been able to participate in the market rally over recent months and recovered from its prior lows. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 10.3% over the same time frame. Despite the recent market rally, LDP now offers investors a starting dividend yield of approximately 7.2% with monthly payouts. This makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking a high level of income generation within their portfolio.

The fund does trade at a discount to NAV valuation at this time, but I don't believe this automatically means that it's an attractive time to accumulate shares. After reviewing the latest semi-annual report, I believe that the fund has some places where it can improve its fundamentals. Additionally, LDP utilizes a large amount of leverage, which has caused the fund's share price to remain sensitive to the higher interest rate environment. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that LDP implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, LDP has total managed assets of $956M that is spread across approximately 266 different holdings. Management searches for value driven opportunities in preferred and income securities, while prioritizing a high current income and the potential for capital appreciation. As the fund's name implies, LDP also attempts to reduce its sensitivity to interest rates by allocating its portfolio to lower duration investments. LDP also provides global exposure to regions outside of the US. The breakdown by its top regions are as follows:

  • United States: 48%
  • Canada: 11%
  • United Kingdom: 10%
  • Other: 9%
  • France: 8%
  • Spain: 4%
  • Switzerland: 4%
  • Japan: 3%
  • Netherlands: 3%

Looking at the breakdown by sector below, we can see that LDP is most heavily allocated to Banking securities at 56% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Insurance and Utilities securities, which accounts for 14% and 10% of assets respectively. Since the majority of the portfolio is allocated to Banking holdings, this presents a level of concentration risk to its holdings.

When looking at the top holdings below, we can see many recognizable large-cap companies. For instance, Goldman Sachs (GS) is the largest holding at 1.7% of assets. This is followed by other high quality names such as Citigroup (C), Bank of America (BAC), and Charles Schwab (SCHW) just to name a few. Despite the exposure to so many different companies, investors must remember that LDP maintains its exposure through preferred securities, so capital appreciation potential is limited.

To demonstrate this weakness, I pulled a performance chart of LDP over the last decade. As we can see below, LDP's share price has declined by about 5.3% over the last ten years. When including all distributions that were paid out, the total return jumps up to 107.5% over the same period. Therefore, investors should expect that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions over time.

Valuation

Since LDP is structured as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, LDP now trades at a slight discount to NAV of approximately 3.41%. For reference, LDP has traded at an average discount to NAV of about 4.65% over the last five year period. To better conceptualize the current discount pricing in relation to its historical ten-year range, I added the red line to the graph below. We can see that LDP currently sits within the middle of its historical discount range.

To get better clarity on the fund's internal performance, I reviewed the latest semi-annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.66 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.28 per share. Assuming a similar performance over the remainder of the 2025 period, LDP is aligned to generate a similar level of net investment income to its prior years. LDP's consistent levels of net investment income is an indication that the fund's strategy for generating income is efficient throughout different market cycles and interest rate environments. However, I noticed that net investment income levels aren't sufficient enough to support the distributions on their own.

Therefore, the fund is reliant on positive net realized gains to make up that difference in earnings. The only problem is that net realized gains can be difficult to predict since it relies on positive market conditions, which allows LDP to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Therefore, LDP offers very little downside protection against market declines. This is why we can see that the fund experienced a large net realized loss of $4.19 per share as a result of 2022's market decline. This reliance on positive market momentum ultimately limits the fund's ability to provide a consistent level of NAV growth over time. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $25.99 per share
  • 2021: $25.34 per share
  • 2022: $20.79 per share
  • 2023: $20.53 per share
  • 2024: $21.89 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $22.04 per share

Risks

Despite the fund focusing on short duration securities, LDP has demonstrated an overall sensitivity to shifting interest rates. As we can see below, LDP's share price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near zero levels. Conversely, LDP's share price rapidly declined when interest rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023. As a result, the fund struggled to regain enough positive momentum to get back to its pre-pandemic levels. Although the Fed recently implemented a small 25 basis point cut to rates, they still remain at the higher end of their range over the last decade.

I believe this sensitivity can be attributed to the fund's use of leverage and exposure to securities with low credit ratings. The fund currently utilizes leverage amounting to 33% of managed assets. 85% of this leverage is structured as fixed rate debt at a weighted average rate of 1.5%. The remaining bulk of leverage is structured as floating rate debt at a variable rate of 5.2%. Investors must also consider the risk that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated.

Additionally, LDP's exposure to securities with poor credit ratings can increase the probability of the fund being impacted by borrower defaults. For instance, S&P Global estimates that the rate of leveraged loan defaults can increase as high as 3.5% by March of 2026, assuming slower economic growth and continued trade disruption related to tariffs.

For reference, securities with credit ratings below a BBB are classified as below investment grade. These are typically borrowers that are more reliant on debt financing to support operations and may have less consistent cash flows. The breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 16%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 70%
  • Not Rated: 12%

Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.131 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.2%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of approximately $1.572 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report, LDP has demonstrated an inconsistent ability to generate a sufficient level of earnings to support the payouts. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $1.32 per share
  • 2021: $1.47 per share
  • 2022: ($2.93) per share
  • 2023: $1.34 per share
  • 2024: $2.93 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.94 per share

As we can see, LDP has only generated a sufficient level of earnings to support payouts in one of the last five years. As a result, we can see how the dividend history below indicates that payouts have trended down over the last decade. Therefore, it becomes difficult to recommend a position in LDP for income investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income. The primary reason that investors maintain exposure to these high yield funds is for the income they can provide. If the income is always under threat of reduction, it defeats the purpose of maintaining a position.

Additionally, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that the majority of the distributions are paid out using net investment income. Therefore, distributions received from LDP would primarily be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the highest tax consequence for investors. So based on your specific tax bracket, the amount of capital invested, or desired income level, a position in LDP may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset the potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that LDP is only a hold for now. Despite the fund trading at a discounted valuation, there are some internal weaknesses that need to be addressed. The fund's reliance on net realized gains means that NAV growth and distribution coverage are dependent on positive market momentum. LDP's use of leverage and exposure to below investment grade securities has also caused LDP to demonstrate a sensitivity to shifts in the interest rate environment. A lower interest rate may eventually create a more favorable environment for the fund, but the appeal is limited for now.

data.title: Sensata Technologies: Resilient Margins, EV Leverage, And Value Still On The Table
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It’s been two months since Sensata Technologies Holdings Inc. (NYSE:ST) released its Q2 2025 results, and what stands out to me is how steady the business has been in the face of weak demand. The company has leaned on margins, cash flow, and high-value niches like aerospace, industrial sensing, and EVs/electrification to offset pressure in autos and heavy vehicles. That balance is why I still see Sensata as mispriced.

Q2 Results: Segment Performance

Here, I’ll quickly run through Sensata’s Q2 2025 results. Sales came in at $943 million, down almost 9% from last year, but still ahead of guidance. Adjusted operating margin held steady at 19%, flat year over year and 70 basis points stronger than Q1. Free cash flow was $116 million, a big improvement from last year, with conversion at 91%. For me, that consistency is the part the market overlooks.

Performance Sensing, which makes up the bulk of the business, posted $652 million in revenue, down 10% year over year as auto and heavy-vehicle volumes stayed soft. Margins here were 22.5%, which is lower than the group average but still solid given the cycle. At the same time, the volume pressure makes it hard to see growth in the near term. In my opinion, this is the side of the business that keeps the stock in the “cyclical auto” bucket for a lot of investors.

On to Sensing Solutions. Revenue here grew 9% year over year to $291 million with operating margins above 30%. That business is tied to aerospace and industrial markets, which are more stable and have higher returns. It now makes up almost a third of total revenue, and I see it as the piece that can pull consolidated profitability higher over time. The resilience here is part of why margins for the whole company held steady even as autos slipped.

Then there’s electrification. Sensata isn’t a pure-play EV supplier, but the company’s content-per-vehicle is increasing as OEMs are getting deeper into electrified platforms. Just think about it: battery management, thermal sensing, and high-voltage protection systems are all areas where it already has exposure, and each new EV platform is an opportunity for the company to grow its wallet share. Yes, I know that the timing is lumpy, but the TAM expansion is real, and even a small increase in EV adoption adds incremental growth that the current valuation doesn’t reflect.

If you carefully consider the trends in and the mix of these segments, you’ll see why I remain bullish on the company in the long term. Performance Sensing will rise and fall with the cycles, but Sensing Solutions and EV content give the business a higher floor and more long-term leverage than the market is pricing in.

I want to touch on capital allocation here, although we’ll look at more numbers in the next section. The company paid out its $0.12 dividend and repurchased about $20 million in stock, and that brings total buybacks to $120 million so far this fiscal year. I’d say that the main takeaway from this quarter is that Sensata is building a floor under earnings, and we can see that the mix shift toward higher-value businesses is more visible.

The Long-Term Trends Support A Rerating In the Near Future

When I zoom out and consider Sensata’s financials from a bird’s-eye view, I see a company that the market is discounting as cyclical. It’s amusing because the numbers say something different, so let’s review them. Over the last decade, revenue has been stable at 3-4 billion, although it peaked at $4.05 billion in 2023 and has slipped to around $3.7 billion on a trailing basis. Conversely, the company’s operating margins have consistently held in the mid to high teens in the same period, although there have been some less-than-stellar margin dips, too.

Coming back to Q2, gross margin sat at 30% and adjusted operating margin at 19%. For a company that is currently trading around 1x sales, it definitely looks like that profitability is being undervalued. We can also see that leverage is still on the higher side, but it’s moving in the right direction: net debt to EBITDA is now 3x, down from closer to 5x a few years ago. Now that interest coverage is around 3–4x, it is a comfortable ratio for a business with steady free cash flow. I’d say that liquidity looks healthy too, with $662 million in cash and a current ratio of about 3x. All things considered, I’d say the balance sheet is in good shape.

Where I think the disconnect lies is in returns. Let’s start with return-on-equity (ROE), which has slipped to the low single digits (currently ~3%), down from 10- 20%+%+ at prior peaks (like in 2018 or 2021), and return-on-invested-capital (ROIC) is around 6% against a cost of capital closer to 8–9%. I believe that the gap is why the stock looks cheap, because the market sees below-WACC returns and assumes the equity isn’t worth paying up for. At the same time, I’m firmly of the opinion that that position misses the point because Sensata is holding profitability in a downcycle, improving its cash conversion, and slowly bringing leverage down. Why? Well, all that needs to happen is for volumes to recover or for EV content to ramp faster, and those returns will improve quickly, and the multiples follow.

Today, Sensata is trading at 14.6x forward earnings and ~10x EV/EBITDA, a valuation that really focuses on the weak parts of the company’s narrative. Remember, everything we’ve seen so far tells us that this is a company with strong operational and financial foundations.

Risks

The biggest risk I see is still exposure to autos and heavy vehicles. Performance Sensing makes up more than two-thirds of revenue, and when volumes stay weak, it drags the whole business. The strong margins help, but they don’t erase the cyclicality.

Debt is another point I can’t ignore. Net leverage has come down, but at 3x EBITDA, it’s still on the high side. Management said the next target is 2.5x, and it’d definitely help. Yes, the company is doing well on interest coverage too (it is improving), but if earnings drop more than expected, that comfort margin can narrow quickly.

It’s also pertinent to highlight how geography adds another layer of risk. China is an important market for Sensata, and any slowdown in demand or trade frictions there can weigh harder than investors are pricing in. I think the same goes for Europe, where industrial demand has been uneven.

Finally, execution in electrification is a factor. Sensata has real opportunities to grow content per vehicle, but the adoption curve isn’t exactly straightforward, and competition is intense. If those wins take longer to materialize, we may see the market continue to value the company as a cyclical auto supplier rather than giving it credit for the optionality.

Investor Takeaway

Sensata is proving it can defend profitability and cash flow even in such a weak cycle. The numbers are good, and Sensing Solutions is showing the kind of growth and stickiness the market overlooks. Now that the price is barely 1x sales and at a big discount to comparable companies, I believe that the stock already prices in the downside but not the upside from mix improvement and EV content gains. For me, that makes it a Buy.

data.title: Saudi Arabia Boosts Oil Output; Global Prices Take A Hit
data.description:
Saudi Arabia has recently increased its crude oil production to reclaim market share rather than maintain price stability. This strategic pivot creates ripple effects throughout global energy markets, affecting producers and consumers worldwide.

The Kingdom’s Market Share Strategy

OPEC’s influence has diminished substantially over the past decade. The organization’s market share fell from 40% to under 25% in recent years. In response, Saudi Arabia began unwinding production cuts ahead of schedule, starting in April 2025.

The Kingdom enjoys a significant competitive advantage with remarkably low production costs compared to other producers. This cost efficiency allows Saudi Arabia to withstand extended periods of lower prices while putting pressure on higher-cost producers.

The strategy serves multiple purposes: reclaiming market share, disciplining quota violations within OPEC+ and asserting Saudi leadership within the organization. However, this approach creates fiscal challenges for the Kingdom, which requires higher oil prices to balance its budget.

US Shale Faces Mounting Pressure

Many US producers have reduced capital spending and drilling activity in response to lower prices. As Travis Stice, CEO of Diamondback Energy (FANG), aptly stated: “We are taking our foot off the accelerator as we approach a red light.” Production from US lower 48 states has already begun declining from recent peaks, potentially marking the first annual drop since the fracking boom began.

Global Economic Impacts

The current market dynamics reveal a significant supply-demand imbalance. Forecasts suggest prices will remain under pressure through 2026 as production increases continue while inventory builds grow.

This price environment creates winners and losers. Major oil exporters face budget pressures, while oil-importing economies benefit from reduced energy costs. Lower oil prices function similar to tax cuts for importing nations such as India, Japan and European countries, easing inflationary pressures and improving trade balances.

Investment Implications

Saudi Arabia’s production strategy triggers long-term structural changes in global energy markets. The Kingdom accepts short-term fiscal challenges to force consolidation across the energy sector as higher-cost producers exit the market.

For investors, this environment requires careful positioning. Companies with low production costs and strong balance sheets can weather the challenging price environment, while high-cost producers face operational difficulties until market conditions improve.

Oil-importing economies stand to benefit from sustained lower prices. The current price cycle will ultimately determine which energy companies maintain sustainable operations through this period of market adjustment.

data.title: Eurozone Bank Lending Remains Unshaken By Global Uncertainty For Now
data.description:
By Bert Colijn

What today’s bank lending data indicates is that the transmission of monetary policy seems to be working pretty well despite all the global uncertainty. Worries about faltering investment - driven by businesses seeking more clarity on the economic outlook - seem to be overstated looking at today’s numbers. Borrowing continues at a decent enough pace to expect economic growth to continue to be supported, albeit at a moderate pace.

Bank lending growth continues on its upward trend, according to the most recent figures. Adjusted loans to households increased by 2.5% year-on-year in August, up from 2.4% in July. Loans to non-financial corporates increased by 3% year-on-year, up from 2.8% in July.

Money growth in the eurozone was previously supported by strong increases in net external assets, but in recent months this has slowed. This is in part because of a lower trade surplus. While the narrow definition of money (M1) shows stable growth at 5% year-on-year, the broader definition (M3) is showing slowing annual growth.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
