data.title: Corporacion America Airports: A Hidden Gem With 11% IRR Potential
data.description:
Corporación América Airports S.A. (NYSE:CAAP) is one of the largest airport operators in the world, operating more than 50 airports around the globe. But there are a lot of uncertainties surrounding this case, as most of its airports are located in Argentina, a country with a very volatile and uncertain macro landscape.

Even so, the structure of the business model, full of moats and protective contracts, together with an attractive valuation, allowing for a double-digit IRR, leads me to assign a buy rating to CAAP stock.

Airports Are Almost Like The Railway Business

A few weeks ago, I analyzed Canadian National Railway (CNI) and mentioned how this type of business model has moats and, as a result, can achieve high cash flow predictability, allowing us to envision compounding and an attractive IRR, such as 8.5% in the case of CNI.

There are interesting parallels between airports and railways. The first and most obvious is that they are assets heavily focused on infrastructure. They are real assets that take a long time to complete, are located in strategic locations, and require massive investment. This creates such a strong barrier to entry that it makes both industries oligopolistic.

There is even some competition in this industry, as is the case with Ezeiza and Jorge Newbery Airport, both in Buenos Aires, Argentina. But in addition to both airports being operated by Corporación América, their focuses are slightly different, which is why they were built, as demand supports both airports.

Ezeiza, being further away and larger, ends up being focused on international flights, while Jorge Newbery is more related to regional flights (generally speaking).

The model of operations between railways and airports also shares another similarity, which is that they are generally carried out through concessions, inflation-linked or with some long-term contract, and especially with a lot of regulation, which is another point that makes the barrier to entry even higher.

There are also optionalities through added services. While railways have contracts and generate additional revenue through terminals, services, storage, and the like, airports have other revenues besides tariffs, which are mainly commercial revenues, rentals, duty-free shops, and the like.

Of course, I must also make it clear that there are important differences between the two sectors. Railways are much less volatile, since even though some are a little more cyclical, airports, especially in countries such as Argentina and Brazil, are very sensitive to macroeconomic changes such as inflation, interest rates, unemployment, and exchange rate changes, since the main costs of flights are in dollars, so in more pressured environments, demand for airports and consumption at airports decrease.

For this reason, multiples and market perception are slightly different, since even though they are businesses with moats and a certain degree of predictability, railway companies are generally seen as something closer to premium utilities, while airports generally trade at more compressed multiples, as is the case with CAAP, since there is still the additional challenge of operating mainly in emerging markets.

Understanding the CAAP Thesis Better

Returning to the specifics of the CAAP thesis, the size of the operating figures is already impressive. There were 79 million passengers served in 2024, with 42 million in Argentina through the 37 airports that the company owns in the country.

The image below also reinforces two important points. The first is that Argentina accounts for about 50% of all EBITDA, and in Q2, it accounted for about 53% of revenue. More important for shareholders are the last squares below each country, which show how long-term the concessions are (and many with the potential to be extended), with the shortest being in Armenia, ending in 2032.

It is also worth mentioning that 68% of its revenue is under the “single till” regime (Argentina and Armenia), which guarantees that a certain IRR must be achieved during the concession period through tariffs, fees, and similar measures. The second largest regime, representing 24% of revenue and 27% of EBITDA, in countries such as Brazil, Uruguay, and Ecuador, is inflation-based, meaning there is no defined IRR, but tariffs are adjusted annually. Finally, the company also works with the Dual Till regime, which is based on WACC to measure tariffs, but the company only operates this way in Italy.

But what is interesting is that, in addition to this revenue, which is constantly increasing due to concessions, the company also has a lot to gain from commercial revenue and margin gains. In Q2, the company increased its margin by more than 1pp, driven in large part by operational leverage in Argentina, even with inflation and a very complex landscape putting pressure on costs. In other words, the company is not only able to increase its top line, but also to transform this into better profitability.

Other points also support the view that there is a lot of growth to come. From 2010 to 2019, the CAGR of total traffic was 11.3%, reaching 84 million in 2019. Since the pandemic, the company has not been able to return to this number, with the maximum reaching 81 million in 2023.

In my view, this is a double-edged sword. On the one hand, it shows how complex the environment in which the company operates is. In Brazil, for instance, traffic is still below 2019 levels. On the other hand, it also shows how much pent-up demand there is in these countries. The Argentine scenario is still under pressure, as is Brazil (with interest rates above 15%). An improvement in this scenario could unlock a lot of demand for the company's airports. Furthermore, in the longer term, the trend is even more stable, with potential for expansion of the middle class and the trend of prioritizing travel experiences and the like.

Finally, it is worth mentioning CAAP's projects. Like a railway, expansions here take time and a lot of capital, but they also unlock value for shareholders if executed well. Zvartnots Airport in Armenia has a total investment of $425 million, which expands capacity by 6.5 million passengers, unlocking significant revenue potential (more capacity for flights, more stores, more sales, more satisfaction for shareholders, and so on).

Being Careful With CAAP’s Assumptions

When I went to Argentina in October 2024, 1,000 Argentine pesos were worth about $1. Now, 1,000 pesos are worth about $0.75. For a period of less than a year, a devaluation of almost 30% is very substantial. And even though CAAP's operations are generally protected contractually by inflation or some IRR, this type of macroeconomic risk can still greatly affect the company, and this on both revenue and margin, such as a significant drop in demand for flights and also decreasing commercial revenue, as well as increasing costs due to higher inflation.

For this reason, and because the forecast is in USD, I will be extremely cautious in my assumptions. For 2026 and 2027, I assumed slightly higher revenue growth, based on the evidence from the last quarter and the fact that short-term projects have more visibility, although the landscape remains complex. From then on, revenue growth gradually declines to only 3% per year (which would basically be inflation plus a small growth, practically without considering expansions or CAAP's growth potential).

For the margin, I considered a constant 11%.

It is worth mentioning that this exercise is exceptionally conservative, since CAAP's net income is generally lower than its operating cash flow and its free cash flow, meaning that the true shareholder value should be higher than that.

This implies a price-to-earnings ratio of 14x for 2026, above market estimates, showing how conservative the assumptions are.

Even so, using a discount rate of 10% (4% risk-free rate + 6% equity risk premium given the higher risk) on this net income stream, and a terminal growth rate of 3%, we arrive at a fair market cap of $3.5 billion for CAAP stock. This implies an upside of 15% and also shows that in this scenario, the IRR would be around 11%, even considering a very subdued scenario.

CAAP's multiples are also substantially lower than those of its peers (mainly due to Argentina's representativeness in its operations). While companies such as Grupo Aeroportuario del Centro Norte (OMAB), Grupo Aeroportuario del Sureste (ASR), Grupo Aeroportuario del Pacifico (PAC), and Aena (OTCPK:ANYYY) are trading between 14.6x and 19.9x earnings for 2026, CAAP is trading at only 8x. The same is true for multiples such as price-to-sales and EV-to-EBITDA, with the company presenting a substantially lower multiple.

The Bottom Line

I was not familiar with CAAP's operations, but the company managed to surprise me positively. Even facing some uncertainties that deserve to be considered in the investment case, the company has a solid business model, with moats and growth triggers for the coming years, which makes it possible to envision an interesting total return for CAAP stock.

Therefore, my rating for CAAP stock is a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
